branch.description: Current branch of the worktree (`la/session-<short_sid>` for daemon-provisioned worktrees).

base_branch.description: Base branch the worktree was forked from when created. `None` when the session row no longer carries that hint (e.g. archived).

head.description: Resolved tip SHA of the worktree's branch (40-char hex). Empty string when the branch has no commits yet.

ahead.description: Commits on `branch` not reachable from `base_branch`. `0` until the first commit; cheap proxy for "anything to merge upstream".

behind.description: Commits on `base_branch` not reachable from `branch`. `0` for freshly-created worktrees.

generated_at.description: RFC3339 timestamp the snapshot was taken. Used by the TUI to suppress redundant re-renders when nothing changed.

WorktreeFileEntry.description: Lightweight per-file summary returned by `worktree.status`. The `staged_hunks` / `unstaged_hunks` counters let the TUI render fold badges without a follow-up `worktree.diff` per file.

path.description: Path relative to the worktree root, forward-slash separated.

old_path.description: Source path when `status == Renamed | Copied`; `None` otherwise.

staged_hunks.description: Number of hunks currently in the index for this file. `0` for purely unstaged changes.

unstaged_hunks.description: Number of hunks currently in the working tree but not the index.

size_bytes.description: On-disk size of the working-tree copy (0 for `Deleted`). Lets the TUI render a size hint and pre-empts loading the diff on huge files.

mode_change.description: `(old_mode, new_mode)` octal — only set when the file's mode bits changed across the diff (chmod +x). `None` otherwise.

WorktreeFileStatus.description: On-disk classification for a single file in a `worktree.status` result. Matches the standard git porcelain v2 vocabulary minus `Unknown`.

oneOf.description: Either side of an unresolved merge conflict.

WorktreeFileKind.description: What kind of payload a file carries. The TUI renders binaries / submodules differently and refuses hunk-level operations on them.
